page: Vultr voc-c-4c-8gb-150s-amd is an instance within the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring an AMD EPYC Genoa processor built on the x86_64 architecture. It provides 4 vCPUs mapped to 4 physical cores with a single thread per core, operating at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The CPU allocation is shared. Memory capacity is configured at 8.0 GB, yielding 2.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is bundled with 150 GB of local NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU hardware is present on this server type.

Benchmark results show strong single-core CPU performance in stress-ng tests, placing in the top 25%. However, multi-core CPU performance, PassMark CPU scores, and memory bandwidth tests for both small and large blocks fall within the average range. Cryptographic and vector instruction capabilities are supported via hardware flags like AVX-512 and AES. Tradeoffs are evident in multi-threaded decompression, database operations, and static web serving throughput, which all register in the bottom 25% of benchmarked systems. Large-model LLM inference is also limited, with medium 7B model text generation performing weakly.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a balanced entry point for compute-focused workloads that require modern AMD EPYC Genoa architecture and NVMe storage without the premium of dedicated CPU cores. Given the 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and the shared CPU allocation, it is best suited for single-threaded processing tasks, lightweight containerized applications, development environments, and basic web utilities that do not demand sustained multi-core execution or high-throughput database operations.
description: Vultr voc-c-4c-8gb-150s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance powered by an AMD EPYC Genoa processor on the x86_64 architecture. It features 4 shared vCPUs, 8.0 GB of RAM, and 150 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. While single-core CPU performance is strong, ranking in the top 25% in stress-ng benchmarks, multi-core performance and memory bandwidth remain average. Tradeoffs include weak multi-threaded decompression, database operations, and static web serving throughput. Lacking a GPU, this instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads requiring modern CPU instructions like AVX-512 without dedicated resource guarantees. It is best suited for single-threaded processing, lightweight containerized applications, and development environments.

tagline: A shared-core AMD EPYC Genoa instance with local NVMe storage, delivering strong single-core performance for lightweight compute workloads.
